attrs.alt: Design Review Checklist - six domain structure with gate bar

content.text: Purpose of the Design Review Checklist

A design review for an agentic AI feature is more complex than a standard product review. The reviewer must evaluate six distinct domains: scope and capability definition, safety and harm mitigation, user experience quality, technical feasibility, observability and evaluation infrastructure, and legal and compliance standing. Checking only the visible user experience misses the failure modes that most often surface in production.

This checklist is a structured gate, not a rubber stamp. Every item on it represents a class of production failure that teams have shipped without catching. The goal is not bureaucratic overhead but to ensure that every significant design decision has been made consciously rather than by default.

The checklist is most effective when completed during a synchronous review meeting rather than asynchronously. Asynchronous completion lets reviewers mark items as done without the cross-domain discussion that reveals interdependencies. A scope decision often has safety and compliance implications that are only visible when domain experts are in the same room.
Domain 1: Scope and Capability

Scope definition is the most common gap in agentic AI design documents. Teams describe what the agent will do in aspirational terms without specifying what it will not do. The boundary between in-scope and out-of-scope determines the agent's refusal behaviour, which directly affects user trust. A clear written scope list is not optional.
Five in, three out minimum:
the scope list must enumerate at least five specific things the agent handles and at least three things it does not. Vague statements like 'handles customer service' do not meet this bar.
Tool call justification:
every tool call — external API, database read, file write — must have a stated user benefit and a documented failure handling path. Undocumented tool calls are scope violations waiting to happen.
Fallback coverage:
every failure path in the journey map must have a designed fallback response. Silent failure or raw error text shown to users are not acceptable fallbacks.
Measurable success criteria:
a criterion like 'user successfully books a meeting within three turns' is measurable. 'User is helped with scheduling' is not. Unmeasurable criteria cannot be tested.
MVP discipline:
scope creep is lethal to agentic AI launches. Phase-2 features must be explicitly named and excluded from the current build scope to prevent the engineering team from inadvertently building them.
Domain 2: Safety and Harm Mitigation

Safety review for an agentic AI feature is not a checklist of prohibited topics. It is an analysis of what could go wrong when the agent operates at scale with real users sending adversarial or unexpected inputs. The review must address output harms, data privacy, prompt injection, and the scope of autonomous action the agent is permitted to take.
Harm category mapping:

identify the specific harm categories relevant to this feature: misinformation, financial harm, privacy exposure, psychological harm, or sector-specific categories. Generic 'no harmful content' instructions are insufficient.
Refusal condition specification:
the system prompt must explicitly state what the agent refuses and what phrasing it uses when refusing. A warm redirect has significantly higher trust ratings than a cold refusal.
PII handling:

personally identifiable information must not flow through the model context unless explicitly required. Mask or tokenise PII fields before injection. The review must verify this is designed, not assumed.
Human-in-the-loop threshold:
define the threshold above which the agent must seek user confirmation before proceeding. For actions that are irreversible, expensive, or high-stakes, the agent should always confirm.
Prompt injection analysis:
any agent that reads user-supplied content — documents, emails, web pages — is vulnerable to prompt injection. The design must document what defences exist at the system prompt and API filter layers.
Autonomous action limits:

the agent should not be permitted to execute financial transactions, send external communications, or delete data without explicit user confirmation. Autonomy is earned through a track record in production, not granted at design time.
Domain 3: User Experience

The UX domain of the design review verifies that prototype testing evidence exists and meets the convergence gates before the engineering build begins. Design sign-off without prototype test results is speculative; with results it is evidence-based.
Wireframe review:

conversation flow wireframes must be reviewed and approved by the design lead. Wireframes that have never been reviewed by a designer are a common source of interaction failures that are expensive to fix post-build.
Prototype test gate:
task completion rate from rapid prototype testing must be at least 80% before sign-off. Below 80% means the agent fails real users more than one time in five on the primary use case.
Trust rating gate:
mean user trust rating from debrief must be at least 3.8 out of 5.0. Trust below 3.0 is a stop signal — users will not return to an agent they do not trust, regardless of technical accuracy.
Clarification rate:
the agent should ask for clarification at most once per session on a well-scoped primary task. Higher rates signal that the system prompt is underspecified.
Accessibility:
output format must be compatible with screen readers. Avoid response patterns that rely on visual structure alone to convey meaning. Markdown tables should be accompanied by prose summaries.
Domain 4: Technical Feasibility
Technical feasibility review for agentic AI covers latency, cost, context management, and infrastructure — the four areas where teams consistently underestimate complexity at design time.
Latency budget:

agree on a P95 latency target for the first token and the full response. For synchronous conversational agents, users begin to perceive delay at two seconds; at four seconds trust degrades measurably.
Context window strategy:
sessions that exceed the model's context window silently truncate older turns. The design must specify what the agent does when this occurs: summarise, re-inject key context, or alert the user.
Token cost estimation:
compute cost per session at the 10th, 50th, and 90th percentile of expected conversation length. Cost surprises post-launch are among the most common reasons AI features are emergency-disabled.
Tool call failure handling:
the review must verify that partial tool call failures — timeout, empty result, malformed response — have designed handling paths. Never assume tools always succeed.
Rate limit strategy:
model APIs impose rate limits that vary by tier. The design must specify retry logic with exponential back-off and the user-facing behaviour during degraded service.
Streaming vs batch:
streaming first-token delivery dramatically improves perceived responsiveness but requires different error handling. The choice must be made consciously and documented.
Domain 5: Observability and Evaluation

Many AI features ship with solid product thinking and weak observability. The result is that problems are discovered through support tickets rather than dashboards. Observability requirements must be designed before build, not added as an afterthought after launch.
Trace ID propagation:

every agent session must carry a trace ID that links all tool calls, model responses, and user turns. Without this, debugging a reported failure requires searching logs by time range, which is impractical at scale.
Metrics agreement:
product, engineering, and data teams must agree before build on which metrics will be tracked, their target values, and who owns each metric.
Eval suite integration:
the automated evaluation suite built from prototype test tasks must be integrated into the CI/CD pipeline. A deployment that fails the eval suite must not reach production.
Logging consent:
conversation logging for debugging and evaluation improvement requires explicit user consent in most jurisdictions. The retention period and access controls must be specified in the design.
Canary rollout:

agentic AI features should not go from 0% to 100% traffic in one step. The rollout plan must specify traffic split increments, the metrics that confirm each increment is safe, and the rollback trigger.
Domain 6: Compliance and Legal

The compliance domain is often deferred until the feature is nearly complete, at which point legal changes require expensive rework. Completing the compliance review at design time is far less costly.
Data residency:

model API calls transmit user data to the model provider's infrastructure. Enterprise and regulated-sector customers have contractual data residency requirements that may prohibit this. Verify compliance before build.
AI disclosure:
the EU AI Act, UK AI regulation, and several US state laws require that users be informed when they are interacting with an AI system. The design must specify where and how this disclosure appears.
Provider Terms of Service:
model providers restrict commercial use cases in ways specific to the model tier. Review the ToS for the model you plan to deploy, not the model you used in prototyping — they may differ.
Training data opt-out:
if the provider uses API conversations to improve future models, customer data may be included by default. Configure opt-out where supported and document the configuration.
Sector regulation:

healthcare, financial services, and legal services each have sector-specific AI regulations beyond general data protection law. A financial advice agent has different obligations than a general customer service agent.
The Design Review Checklist

Use this checklist during the review meeting. Every unchecked item represents a RED status for its domain. The gate requires all domains to be GREEN or AMBER with a documented plan before design sign-off proceeds.

# design-review-checklist-genai.md
# Complete during the review meeting.
# Status: GREEN (done), AMBER (deferred with plan), RED (unresolved)
# A single RED blocks the design sign-off gate.

## Domain 1: Scope and Capability
[ ] Capability scope: at least 5 in-scope and 3 out-of-scope items documented in PRD
[ ] Every tool call justified with explicit user benefit
[ ] Fallback response designed for every documented failure path
[ ] Human escalation path identified for low-confidence outputs
[ ] No product copy claims unverified capabilities
[ ] Task completion success criterion measurable (not vague "agent helps user")
[ ] MVP scope separated from phase-2 enhancements in PRD

## Domain 2: Safety and Harm
[ ] Harm categories relevant to this feature documented
[ ] Explicit refusal conditions in system prompt spec
[ ] Output guardrails described (content filter, confidence threshold)
[ ] PII fields masked or tokenised before entering model context
[ ] Human-in-the-loop threshold defined for high-stakes actions
[ ] Prompt injection attack surface analysed; mitigations specified
[ ] Agent cannot autonomously execute irreversible actions without user confirmation

## Domain 3: User Experience
[ ] Conversation flow wireframes reviewed and approved by design lead
[ ] Rapid prototype testing complete: task completion rate >= 80%
[ ] User trust rating from debrief >= 3.8 / 5.0
[ ] Average clarification requests per session <= 1.0
[ ] Scope violations per session <= 0.5
[ ] Error and refusal messages written in plain language
[ ] Response format accessible to screen readers

## Domain 4: Technical Feasibility
[ ] P95 latency target set for first token and full response
[ ] Context window management strategy for long sessions
[ ] Token cost estimate per session at p10, p50, p90
[ ] Tool call partial-failure handling designed
[ ] Retry logic with exponential back-off specified
[ ] Streaming vs batch response decision documented
[ ] Load test approach defined before traffic ramp

## Domain 5: Observability and Evaluation
[ ] Trace ID propagated through every agent turn
[ ] Key product metrics agreed with target values (TCR, CRR, SVC, UTR)
[ ] Alerting thresholds set for eval degradation and error rate
[ ] Eval suite integrated into CI/CD; deployment blocked on regression
[ ] Conversation logging consent and retention policy defined
[ ] Monitoring dashboards specified before launch
[ ] Canary rollout plan: split, criteria, rollback trigger

## Domain 6: Compliance and Legal
[ ] Data residency: model API calls comply with customer data location rules
[ ] AI disclosure: users informed they interact with an AI system
[ ] Model provider Terms of Service reviewed for commercial restrictions
[ ] Training data opt-out configured if provider supports it
[ ] Sector-specific regulation mapped (healthcare, finance, legal)
[ ] Privacy policy updated to reflect AI-assisted processing
[ ] Legal team sign-off obtained; ticket reference in PRD

## Sign-Off Summary
  Reviewer:
  Date:
  Domain statuses: Scope[ ] Safety[ ] UX[ ] Tech[ ] Obs[ ] Legal[ ]
  Decision: APPROVED / CONDITIONAL / BLOCKED
  Conditions or blocked items:
Automating Gate Checks

For teams running frequent design reviews across multiple features, automating the gate check reduces manual error and makes the gate auditable. The following script reads a completed checklist markdown file and reports domain status, exiting with a non-zero code if any domain is red:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# design_review_check.py
# Reads a completed checklist markdown and reports per-domain status.
# Exits 0 if all domains GREEN, exits 1 if any domain is RED.
# Usage: python design_review_check.py design-review-checklist-genai.md

import sys
import re
from collections import defaultdict

DOMAINS = [
    "Domain 1: Scope and Capability",
    "Domain 2: Safety and Harm",
    "Domain 3: User Experience",
    "Domain 4: Technical Feasibility",
    "Domain 5: Observability and Evaluation",
    "Domain 6: Compliance and Legal",
]

def parse_checklist(path):
    with open(path) as f:
        lines = f.readlines()

    domain_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {"checked": 0, "unchecked": 0})
    current_domain = None

    for line in lines:
        stripped = line.strip()
        for d in DOMAINS:
            if stripped.startswith("## " + d):
                current_domain = d
                break
        if current_domain is None:
            continue
        if re.match(r"^\[x\]", stripped, re.I) or re.match(r"^\[X\]", stripped):
            domain_stats[current_domain]["checked"] += 1
        elif re.match(r"^\[ \]", stripped):
            domain_stats[current_domain]["unchecked"] += 1

    return domain_stats

def domain_status(stats):
    total = stats["checked"] + stats["unchecked"]
    if total == 0:
        return "EMPTY"
    return "GREEN" if stats["unchecked"] == 0 else "RED"

def main():
    if len(sys.argv) < 2:
        print("Usage: python design_review_check.py <checklist.md>")
        sys.exit(2)

    path = sys.argv[1]
    domain_stats = parse_checklist(path)

    any_red = False
    print(f"\nDesign Review Gate -- {path}\n" + "-" * 50)
    for d in DOMAINS:
        st = domain_stats.get(d, {"checked": 0, "unchecked": 0})
        s = domain_status(st)
        checked = st["checked"]
        total = st["checked"] + st["unchecked"]
        icon = "OK" if s == "GREEN" else "FAIL"
        print(f"  [{icon}] {d}: {checked}/{total} items  [{s}]")
        if s == "RED":
            any_red = True

    print()
    if any_red:
        print("RESULT: BLOCKED -- resolve all RED domains before sign-off.")
        sys.exit(1)
    else:
        print("RESULT: APPROVED -- all domains GREEN.")
        sys.exit(0)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Running the Review Meeting

A structured design review meeting for an agentic AI feature takes 60 to 90 minutes and requires five roles: a facilitator who drives through the checklist, a designer who owns the UX domain, an engineer who owns technical feasibility, a product manager who owns scope, and a legal or compliance representative for the compliance domain.

The facilitator reads each checklist item aloud and asks whether it is complete. For contested items, the meeting does not debate the answer in real time — it records the item as AMBER and assigns an owner and due date. The goal of the meeting is to identify gaps, not to resolve them on the spot.

After the meeting, the facilitator publishes the completed checklist with domain statuses and AMBER plans. The design sign-off gate opens when all AMBER items are resolved and verified. A single RED item — one unresolved issue without a plan — keeps the gate closed regardless of the rest of the checklist.
60 minutes is sufficient:
if the review is running over, the design document is incomplete. Stop the review and reschedule when the document is ready.
No surprises in the meeting:
distribute the checklist 48 hours before so reviewers can pre-read and pre-mark items. Meeting time is for discussion, not reading.
Record decisions:
every AMBER item needs an owner, a resolution action, and a due date recorded in the checklist. Verbal agreements do not get followed up.
Gate authority:
designate one person with authority to declare the gate open or closed. Committee decisions without a named decision-maker produce circular conversations that never resolve.
Cross-domain dependencies:

the facilitator must flag items in one domain that depend on decisions in another. A safety decision about PII masking depends on the technical feasibility of masking before injection. These linkages get missed in asynchronous review.
Linking to Downstream Gates

The design review checklist is the first of three sign-off gates in the production lifecycle. Items that pass the design review become requirements that the engineering build must satisfy. Items that are AMBER at design review become acceptance criteria for the UAT gate.

The safety domain of this checklist feeds directly into the safety impact assessment and the risk register. The observability domain becomes the monitoring requirements for the deployment plan. The UX domain's prototype test metrics become the baseline against which the post-launch eval suite is measured.

Each gate is a checkpoint in a continuous evidence chain. The design review is not a standalone bureaucratic exercise — it is the point where design decisions become engineering commitments. The quality of the checklist review determines the quality of what gets built.
